title: 6 Best OfferUp Alternatives in 2025 for Local Selling
metaDescription: Looking for OfferUp alternatives? We ranked 6 platforms that beat OfferUp on fees, privacy, and flexibility for local and shipped sales.
heroImageAlt: A person photographing items on a clean white table to list on a local selling app, with a smartphone showing product listings
content:
6 Best OfferUp Alternatives in 2025 for Local Selling

OfferUp is a perfectly decent marketplace app — nice design, solid local reach, generally trustworthy. But "perfectly decent" isn't always good enough when you're losing 12.9% of every shipped sale, or when you wish you could do something more useful with your proceeds than wait for someone to hand you cash. These six OfferUp alternatives do things better, and we'll tell you exactly how.

At a Glance: 6 OfferUp Alternatives Compared

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly No fees Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Overall
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Huge reach, privacy cost
Craigslist Free Cash Yes No Email anonymization Free but dated
Nextdoor Free Cash Yes No Neighborhood-verified Best for neighbors
Mercari No fees Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Good for shipping
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for rare items

#1 Swapedly — The OfferUp Alternative With a Smarter Fee Model

Here's the thing about OfferUp: the 12.9% fee on shipped transactions is hard to justify when you do the math. Sell a $150 pair of sneakers and you're handing $19.35 to OfferUp. Sell them on Swapedly with a Swapedly charges no transaction fees. Not a dramatic difference there — but sell a $300 camera and the gap widens fast. OfferUp takes $38.70. Swapedly takes $20. That's $18.70 back in your pocket.

But fees are only part of the story. Swapedly's real differentiator is the Swap Bucks (SB) system. Every dollar you earn stays within the ecosystem as purchasing power. Sell something you don't need, earn SB, use those SB to buy something you do need. There's no bank transfer to wait for, no PayPal hold — your proceeds are immediately spendable.

Like OfferUp, Swapedly supports local pickup. Unlike OfferUp, it also handles shipping — and the seller chooses which option to offer. New sellers get a $40 Swap Bucks bonus at signup (grab it at swapedly.com), plus there's a referral program that pays 1 SB per friend who joins.

If you upgrade to Swapedly Plus at $9.99/month, you earn 1.5x SB on every sale and get 5 featured listings per month. That multiplier adds up quickly if you're a regular seller. But honestly, the free tier is genuinely usable — no pressure to subscribe.

Swapedly Pros

  • Swapedly charges no transaction fees. There are simply more buyers on Facebook than on any other local marketplace app. If you're selling something large — a couch, a refrigerator, a car — the audience size matters and Facebook delivers.

    Local transactions are free, which is a genuine advantage. The 5% fee on shipped sales is lower than OfferUp's 12.9%, though still higher than what you'd pay on Swapedly for items under $400. The obvious downside is that every transaction is visible to your Facebook network and tied to your real identity. For many people, that's a dealbreaker.

    Facebook Marketplace Pros

    • Enormous buyer base in every US city
    • Truly free for local cash transactions
    • Easy cross-listing with Facebook groups

    Facebook Marketplace Cons

    • Your real name and photo are visible to every buyer
    • Scam accounts are widespread
    • Facebook data harvesting from all activity

    #3 Craigslist — Free, Scrappy, and Still Standing

    Craigslist has survived long enough to become a kind of monument to the early internet's stubbornness. It's ugly, it's basic, and it's completely free for most categories. For high-value local items like furniture, appliances, or vehicles, it still draws real buyers.

    What Craigslist will never give you: an app worth using, built-in payment processing, buyer or seller protection, or confidence that the person emailing you isn't a bot. It's a tool, not a platform. If you can work with those limitations, the price is right.

    Craigslist Pros

    • Free to list in most categories
    • Good reach in urban markets
    • No account required to post in many cities

    Craigslist Cons

    • No payment protection or dispute resolution
    • Scam listings are a genuine problem
    • Website design frozen in 2003

    #4 Nextdoor — The Safest Local Option

    If your main concern with OfferUp is meeting strangers, Nextdoor solves that problem elegantly: everyone on the platform is your actual neighbor, verified by their address. The trust level is categorically different from any other marketplace. You're not selling to a random user — you're selling to someone who lives on your street.

    The tradeoff is reach. Nextdoor is neighborhood-bound by design, cash-only, and has no shipping support. It's perfect for free stuff, local handoffs, or items that are genuinely too heavy to ship. Beyond that radius, you need a different tool.

    Nextdoor Pros

    • Neighborhood-verified buyers — maximum safety
    • Zero fees, zero overhead
    • Community trust built in

    Nextdoor Cons

    • No shipping capability
    • Limited to immediate neighborhood area
    • Cash only — no payment protection

    #5 Mercari — The Shipping Specialist

    Mercari is the platform that made shipping feel less scary for casual sellers. The app walks you through the process, provides prepaid label options, and handles buyer-seller communication cleanly. The no transaction fee is the same as Swapedly, but without a cap — so it gets progressively more expensive on higher-value items.

    For items under $200 where you want to ship, Mercari is a legitimate alternative to OfferUp. Above $200, the no-cap fee structure starts hurting. On a $400 item, Mercari charges $40; Swapedly charges nothing max.

    Mercari Pros

    • Excellent shipping workflow for beginners
    • Clean, easy-to-use app
    • Strong buyer and seller protection

    Mercari Cons

    • No fee cap — gets expensive on valuable items
    • Buyer service fees can reduce buyer willingness to pay
    • Dispute resolution can be slow

    #6 eBay — The High-Reach, High-Fee Option

    eBay's 13.25% final value fee makes it the most expensive option on this list, and it's not even close for local selling — eBay is almost entirely a shipping platform. But if you have something rare, specialty, or collectible that needs maximum exposure, eBay's global buyer pool is genuinely unmatched.

    For everyday used items? You can almost certainly do better on any of the platforms above eBay on this list. The complexity and fees aren't justified unless you need that global reach.

    eBay Pros

    • Unmatched global buyer reach
    • Auction format excellent for rare items
    • Very strong buyer protection

    eBay Cons

    • 13.25% fee — most expensive on this list
    • No real local selling functionality
    • Complex fee structure with listing upgrades

    Which OfferUp Alternative Is Right for You?

    If you're making the switch from OfferUp, the right alternative depends on what frustrated you most. Annoyed by the fees? Swapedly's capped structure saves money on anything over $155 in value. Worried about privacy? Swapedly doesn't require a social media login. Want to keep selling locally? Swapedly supports local pickup just like OfferUp does.

    For purely local, no-fee selling where you're comfortable with cash, Craigslist or Nextdoor work fine. For nationwide reach with good shipping infrastructure, Mercari is worth a look. But if you want a platform that improves on OfferUp in multiple areas at once, Swapedly is the clear answer.

    The $40 new user gift card doesn't hurt either. See also our roundup of the best Facebook Marketplace alternatives and our breakdown of the best places to sell stuff online in 2025.
